Patient states symptoms started approximately 1 week ago. Wound reportedly started after he was checking his blood sugar. He then continued more pain and swelling In the area presented into the hospital. On April 11 he was diagnosed with cellulitis and had a I&D in the ED. Patient was ordered Bactrim and Keflex are patient states he never actually started the antibiotics. He continued have worsening pain and swelling and return to Henderson today where he was transferred to Valley Hospital after talking with hand surgery. Patient states he has been feeling generally unwell however denies any significant past medical history. Denies any tobacco, alcohol, and recreational drug use. In the ED hand surgery was consulted. Patient underwent a bedside I&D with orthopedic surgery on 4/14. Purulent discharge was noted coming from the eponychial fold. ID was consulted. Blood cultures remain negative. Wound culture showed mixed growth gram-positive flora. Was cleared for discharge by orthopedic surgery recommended outpatient follow-up. Cleared for discharge by ID who recommended Augmentin and doxycycline for 2 weeks. Medications were sent to his pharmacy. Information provided in discharge paperwork. All appropriate refills on medications as well as new prescriptions provided to the patient. Patient educated on importance of medication adherence and reason for medication administration. Given strict return precautions. The patient has no other complaints at this time. All questions addressed at the bedside. The patient is amenable to bedside I&D. The risk, benefits, and complications of the procedure be performed were discussed at length with the patient. Risk include but not limited to pain, bleeding, infection, damage neurovascular structures, damage to tendinous or ligamentous tissues, need for reoperation, continued swelling at the hand, chronic pain, scarring, contracture, stiffness, amongst many others. There is of local anesthesia were additionally discussed to include pain, persistent numbness. With all these things in mind the patient elected to proceed with right index finger distal phalanx bedside I&D Indication Right Index finger Infection Sedation 5 CC 1% plain lidocaine Complications None Technique Patient was greeted at the bedside and appropriate consents were obtained as above. The right hand and Index finger were cleaned with a chlorhexidine solution. The MCP volar crease was injected with 5 CC of 1% plain lidocaine. After adequate anesthesia the area was then cleaned again with additional chlorhexidine solution. Proper sterile technique was used. First using a Freer, the eponychial fold was evacuated. Purulent discharge noted to be coming from eponychial fold. The fingernail was removed of the Index finger for proper evaluation. Sharp dissection was used using a 15 blade to debride any fibrinous material in the center of the wound bed. Dead skin was trimmed around the wound edges with sharp scissors. Afterwards sterile dressing using Xeroform, 4 X 4's, Kerlix, and Ace wrap was placed. Patient tolerated this well.
